author: @AtaruMor0boshi
text:
Pure sine wave inverters (barring frauds - assuming good products from reputable manufacturers) do actually produce beautiful sine waves. Nyquist-Shannon works in both ways, and it is about the *perfect* reconstruction of any given bandwidth-limited signal. Don't fall in the old if-it's-digital-then-it's-jagged argument. Nope. You can reconstruct a perfectly pure (in the real world, to a tiny fraction of a percent) sine wave  from a digital stream double its frequency. Besides, digital topologies may carry great benefits but are not really required.
In fact, many real world devices are specified to <1% THD and some reach unity power factor. That's the kind of output you get, for example, from grid-connected PV inverters or any online double-conversion (VFI) UPS (again, assuming non-scams). You can view most of them a bit as mighty powerful class-D subwoofer amplifiers, or even class-C radio transmitters with a <100Hz bandwidth, depending on their topology. They are available in powers from <1kW to >1MW, with or without galvanic insulation from the DC side, single or three-phase, and they can be quite efficient too (even >99% recently, increasing with the actual power output) and reliable (again, barring garbage).
So-called modified or approximate sine wave inverters or UPSs are a whole other thing: their output doesn't even remotely look like a sine function, but they are much cheaper and acceptable in many cases. Feeding the SMPSU of your average PC, for example. Hint: some hair driers at half power can be a lethal challenge for sub-par products.
Time-domain plots (the classic oscilloscope view) can fool even experts: looking at your inverter output also on a spectrum analyzer (a frequency domain view based on a FFT of the input signal) tells a lot more about the quality of the waveform you are producing, and preferably under different loads (impedance is a /complex/ thing). Maybe your scope has this function or you can unlock it with a license upgrade.

author: @LiLi-or2gm
text: There's some high-end audio amplifiers that use 400Hz AC for power input. The higher frequency allows the use of smaller transformers and lower capacitance values (with low ESR), making it far easier to eliminate ripple, and to react quickly to the high current transients required by the bass drivers.

author: @NorthernKitty
text:
The "false advertising" is astonishing, really.  And, anymore, they don't even try to deny it.

I regularly do product reviews for merchants in exchange for merchandise.  It's managed through an intermediary, so I'm under no obligation to give anyone a "good" review - they don't know who their products are being reviewed by.  In one particular instance, I was reviewing a USB power bank, and it wasn't producing anywhere near the amp-hour rating they advertised.  When I contacted the manufacturer's support line, I was told flat-out, "oh, we inflate that number to match what our competitors advertise... but everybody does that so you'll find ours actually performs better than our competitors."

Needless to say, they didn't get a stellar review from me, and I was sure to include their quote in my review.  😋

author: @jimmycorkhill1390
text: It is great and I was impressed by its simplicity. However, I have 4 of the claimed pure sine wave inverters, the smallest is in a power pack (solar generator) 400wh and is 400W output costing 330 euros. The largest is a 4kW model with combined 4kW solar charge controller and utility charger costing about 450 euros. They all work all electronic and digital stuff perfectly well for years. Unless one could get free or cheap motors to make a true pure inverter, would it be cost effective? I know it would be fun and great experience. Best wishes.

author: @ChristieNel
text: A "pure" sine wave is generated digitally by using a very high frequency PWM into high power MOSFETs (half bridge?), where the PWM duty is modulated using a DAC, which will consequently have the resolution of the DAC. But add an inline inductor or bypass capacitor and it's pretty smooth. But then, once you add a decent load, especially an inductive load, it will distort the shape and frequency of the sine. It's pretty messy stuff. You might use the fans to drive the duty cycle. There's also the start up to consider. If you're starting up, you'll be generating DC initially instead of 50 Hz, which will seriously mess up some devices.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Thats true. Modern inverters will not produce perfect sine waves. In reality though it's good enough. These could do way better but for reasons of switching loses it's not done.
For a function generator, if you do not need a to high frequency ( say up to 1 MHz ) it can be done with digital pwm and a filter, kind of like the inverter and it will be a more perfect sine wave then any generator. I think  it's done like this in modern function generators

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Trully, pure sign waves exist only in math functions. Every signwave in the real world is an approximation with some being very very close others just a rough appearance. 

modified sign wave seen in cheap inverters put out effectivly the same rms  valve a sinewave would over a cycle through a pure resistive load but its behavior with nonlinear loads can be tough on motors and electronics. 

socalled "pure sign wave" inverters use a pulse width modulation and a filtering inductor and capacitor to put out a reasonably good approximate sign wave that most electronics and motors can work normally with.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text:
I think this one is more about proof of concept than efficiency. Now we know the output waveform it's a good project for us to experiment with at home. Rob is about inspiring us to further experiment on his ideas rather than expect him to do the work for us. Is good and well to watch YouTube and see what's possible but unless we actually do what are we really learning?
By the way, I am not saying this is what you do, just saying for others to try these ideas.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
assuming  a motor is 90% and a generator were 90% efficent then together 90%×90%=81%. 

motors and generators performance varies with load but I'm guessing more realistic guess is probably close 85%×85%=72.25% for loads close to the limit.
